title: FAR magicscience 5d Rei
wikitext:
[]: FAR / magic=science / 5d Rei             Quote Post by Meerkat » Wed Sep 10, 2014 6:34 am Am I the only one who is really bothered by the First Ancestral Race? I remember when watching NGE for the first time I was really fascinated by the unusual juxtaposition of science fiction and religious themes (like the simultaneous existence of brain-simulations and souls). I'm an atheist irl but I really think "God" is an interesting concept and I liked how it felt like there was this divine predestination to the main events; this inevitable process that didn't care for the lives or feelings of the characters. Maybe my interpretation was idiosyncratic (I don't think so), but I really liked that theme and for me part of that was the ambiguity as to whether there was a god; whether the events were something beyond scientific understanding. So when I read (on the wiki) about the FAR I got pretty disappointed. Aliens? Really? Not that I would have liked a confirmation of the existence of God in NGE to be confirmed (That would be even worse!). It's just that the Evangelion "lore" was more interesting when there were less answers. Blah. Meaningless rant. Does anyone else feel like this?

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Wed Sep 10, 2014 6:48 am Ask yourself "where did the first ancestral race come from?" and you're back at square one - ignorant and in wonder.

[]:             Quote Post by pwhodges » Wed Sep 10, 2014 6:50 am In any case, having the FAR seeding life on the earth is not so different from many people's conception of God's part in it.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Wed Sep 10, 2014 7:09 am Besides they were already alluded in episode 21, when Gendo explains that the walls of the cavity where is the Black Moon was made artificially.

[]:             Quote Post by ThanatosII » Wed Sep 10, 2014 8:51 am Whether it's God or aliens, it's still a concept beyond our human understanding. I think that's what matters most.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Sep 10, 2014 9:59 am That crap isn't mentioned anywhere in the series proper, it's a cheap cash in to exploit nerds who can't use their imagination and must have answers spoonfed to them. You may freely ignore it.

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Wed Sep 10, 2014 10:09 am Chuckman wrote: That crap isn't mentioned anywhere in the series proper, it's a cheap cash in to exploit nerds who can't use their imagination and must have answers spoonfed to them. You may freely ignore it.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 10, 2014 10:26 am Chuckman wrote: That crap isn't mentioned anywhere in the series proper, it's a cheap cash in to exploit nerds who can't use their imagination and must have answers spoonfed to them. You may freely ignore it.

[]:             Quote Post by Rosenakahara » Wed Sep 10, 2014 10:38 am Chuckman wrote: That crap isn't mentioned anywhere in the series proper, it's a cheap cash in to exploit nerds who can't use their imagination and must have answers spoonfed to them. You may freely ignore it.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:18 pm Bagheera wrote: By that logic there's no need for Final.

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:20 pm Aliens are just other life not from Earth. Nothing "hokey" about that.

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:32 pm Chuckman wrote: Oh, boo. Besides, I don't see how 'aliens did it' is any more or less hokey than magic.

[]:             Quote Post by Sorrow » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:40 pm Any sufficiently advanced magic is indistinguishable from technology.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:41 pm -Arthur C. Clarke

[]:             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:42 pm ^ Touche.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:45 pm Chuckman wrote: Oh, boo. Besides, I don't see how 'aliens did it' is any more or less hokey than magic.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:49 pm Episode 21 is pretty dense as far as info-dumping goes, but GAINAX's history as SF fans shows through ( inter alia) when you get presented as a scientific discipline; so that with Gendo's offhand remark about the GeoFront, they might as well state outright then and there "It's a Forerunner artefact, guys!" About the only thing missing is qualifying Project-E as Applied Theology (the engineering discipline, as opposed to the branch of psychology for second-guessing Transcended entities).

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Sep 10, 2014 12:59 pm Bagheera wrote: Because, bluntly, "it's magic!" is the response of a weak mind to things it doesn't understand. The FAR are a tool the viewer can use to explore both the events of the series and the motivations of some of its most influential actors. Discarding them in favor of a mystical explanation explains nothing, instead simply handwaving all that happens and insisting that no answers are to be found. Why did Yui get in the Eva? Because she's crazy. Why did Seele hatch their plots? Because they're crazy. Why are there prophecies about Angels and the HIP? Because, man, because. It's lazy. Figuring out not only what happens in the show but also how it happens is interesting. Attributing events to magic or divine will or whatever removes that element from the show and offers nothing of substance to replace it -- it thus diminishes the show immeasurably. Far from being hokey, the tech in the show is what makes it not hokey. It's what tells us the show is full of a sense of wonder, and that these people are not crazy and that none of this is actually predestined at all -- instead it just looks that way because the tech's just that freakin' advanced. Clarke's law is in effect here. I think that's pretty cool.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Wed Sep 10, 2014 1:07 pm Chuckman wrote: Seriously? I'd hardly call all of the in-depth analysis I've done a handwave, bub.

[]: Re: I don't like the FAR             Quote Post by ThanatosII » Wed Sep 10, 2014 1:48 pm Meerkat wrote: whether the events were something beyond scientific understanding.
